content.full_text:
CITY OF CALISTOGA

In Beautiful                    Napa Valley

1232 WASHINGTON STREET  •  CALISTOGA, CALIFORNIA 94515  •  (707) 942-5188


August 13, 1990


California Fair Political Practices Commission
Attn: Scott Hallabrin
     Acting General Counsel
P.O.Box 428  Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95804-0807


Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

The City of Calistoga is undergoing a process to update its General Plan.  While the General Plan changes impact the community as a whole, some concerns have been raised regarding the land ownership of a number of members on the Calistoga City Council. What follows is a brief overview of what I feel are the interests which may lead to possible conflicts.

Mr. Robert Maxfield has an owner-interest in a 40 acre +/- piece of property on [illegible] street called Silverado Trail. One ownership interest is in excess of $100,000.00 (closer to $4,000,000.00) and that property is presently suggested in the General Plan as planned development. Mr. Maxfield also has a financial interest in a piece of property adjacent to a City parking lot. The value of his interest is between $10,000.00 and $100,000.00.  Finally Mr. Maxfield has an ownership interest in a piece of residential property, the value is in excess of $100,000.00.  Mr. Maxfield has a rental income in excesses of $10,000.00 each, from the Silverado property and from the lease-hold  property.  Mr. Maxfield's interests can be isolated to specific areas of the City of Calistoga.  My questions regarding Mr. Maxfield's interests are: 1) May he discuss the General Plan in theory and concept, such as in a work shop setting? 2) Is it appropriate to divide the vote on the General Plan in sections of the City so that Mr. Maxfield may vote on the General Plan as it to pertains to sections other  than  those in which he has a financial interest?

Mayor Jim Hughes obtains rental income in excess of $10,000.00
annually from a residential property on Cedar Street.
Additionally, he obtains income between $1,000.00 and $10,000.00
from salary earned by his wife who works for a large local real
estate firm. The same questions which apply to: Mansfield apply
to the Mayor. If we divide the General Plan into sections into
sections may the Mayor vote on sections other than those in which
his residential property is located? Further, does it effect his
vote if the realty company where his wife is employed has
listings for various pieces of property in the City?

The final Councilmember, Mr. Bob Beck, is perhaps the most
complicated. Mr. Beck has investment income, both personal and
business entity, and owns real estate as an individual and as a
business entity in eight pieces of property within the City
limits. His ownership income exceeds $100,000.00 in the eight
pieces of property and his rental income exceeds $10,000.00 in
four of those pieces of property. Mr. Beck also has seven
outstanding loans in excess of $10,000.00 to persons who reside
in various areas of the City. The same questions apply: may Mr.
Beck vote?

I have reviewed section 87103 of the Government Code regarding
the definitions of material financial affect distinguishable from
its affect on the public generally, and find that we are unable
to make an evaluation.

The vote on the General Plan will be coming before the Council
the end of September, and we would appreciate a determination
from your office. If you require any further information, please
contact me by phone so that we may expedite the process.

Sincerely,

Natassha Merkuloff
City Administrator

po
FPPC
